section.title: The Ministry of Reconciliation
section.sectionRationale: This is the theological summit of the letter. Christ's love compels Paul, for he is convinced that One died for all, and therefore all died; those who live should now live for the One who died and was raised for them. Anyone in Christ is a new creation: the old has passed away and the new has come. All this is from God, who reconciled us to Himself through Christ and gave us the ministry and message of reconciliation, so that Paul pleads as an ambassador, be reconciled to God. It closes with the great exchange: God made the sinless Christ to be sin for us, so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God.
section.thesis: Constrained by the love of Christ who died for all so that all died, believers are made new creations and God through Christ reconciles the world to Himself, not counting sins against them; He commits the message of reconciliation to His ambassadors who implore, be reconciled to God, on the ground of the great exchange, that the sinless One was made sin for us so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the love of Christ that compels, for One died for all and all died; the new creation and God's ministry of reconciliation in Christ; and the ambassadors' appeal grounded in the great exchange of 5:21.

units.label: The love of Christ compels us
units.note: One died for all, therefore all died

verses.text: Therefore, since we know what it means to fear the Lord, we try to persuade men. What we are is clear to God, and I hope it is clear to your conscience as well.
We are not commending ourselves to you again. Instead, we are giving you an occasion to be proud of us, so that you can answer those who take pride in appearances rather than in the heart.
If we are out of our mind, it is for God; if we are of sound mind, it is for you.
For Christ’s love compels us, because we are convinced that One died for all, therefore all died.
And He died for all, that those who live should no longer live for themselves, but for Him who died for them and was raised again.

units.label: New creation and reconciliation
units.note: God reconciling the world to Himself in Christ

verses.text: So from now on we regard no one according to the flesh. Although we once regarded Christ in this way, we do so no longer.
Therefore if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away. Behold, the new has come!
All this is from God, who {{katallasso|reconciled}} us to Himself through Christ and gave us the ministry of reconciliation:
that God was reconciling the world to Himself in Christ, not counting men’s trespasses against them. And He has committed to us the message of {{katallage|reconciliation}}.

units.label: Ambassadors and the great exchange
units.note: be reconciled to God; He made Him to be sin for us

verses.text: Therefore we are {{presbeuo|ambassadors}} for Christ, as though God were making His appeal through us. We implore you on behalf of Christ: Be reconciled to God.
God made Him who knew no sin to be sin on our behalf, so that in Him we might become the {{dikaiosyne|righteousness}} of God.

katallasso.gloss: To reconcile, to change from enmity to friendship, to restore a broken relationship. God is the one who reconciles: He takes the initiative, through Christ, to bring estranged sinners back into peace with Himself. Reconciliation here is not two parties meeting halfway but God removing the barrier of sin by not counting trespasses against us. The offended party Himself provides the way back, which is the wonder of the gospel.

katallage.gloss: Reconciliation, the state of restored relationship and the message that proclaims it. God has given both the ministry and the word of reconciliation, so that the accomplished peace is now announced to the world. It is the good news that God in Christ has dealt with the enmity of sin and opens His arms to sinners. The message calls hearers to receive what God has already achieved: be reconciled to God.

presbeuo.gloss: To be an ambassador, to serve as an official envoy who represents a sovereign and speaks with his authority. Believers are ambassadors for Christ, so that God makes His appeal through them; the messenger carries not his own opinion but the King's own word. The dignity and weight of the calling is immense, for to hear the ambassador is to hear God's own plea, be reconciled to Him.

dikaiosyne.gloss: Righteousness, the status of being right with God, and the very righteousness that belongs to God Himself. In the great exchange the sinless Christ was made sin for us so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God. This is not a righteousness we produce but one credited to us in Christ: our sin reckoned to Him, His righteousness reckoned to us. It is the ground of our acceptance and the heart of the gospel.

basic.context: Paul reaches the heart of his defense and of the gospel itself. He does not preach to commend himself but because the love of Christ leaves him no choice: Christ died for all, so all died, and those who live must now live for Him. Anyone united to Christ is a new creation. Behind it all stands God, who reconciled us to Himself through Christ and entrusted the message of reconciliation to His servants. So Paul pleads like an ambassador, be reconciled to God, and grounds the plea in the great exchange, that God made the sinless Christ to be sin for us so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God.
basic.hermeneutics: These verses compress the whole gospel into a few lines, so each phrase repays careful weight. Notice that reconciliation is entirely God's initiative, that new creation language echoes Genesis and the prophets, and that verse 21 states the double imputation at the core of justification. Read the passage as both doctrine, what God has done in Christ, and appeal, the summons to receive it.
basic.originalLanguages: God katallasso, reconciles, sinners to Himself and gives the katallage, the reconciliation, both the accomplished peace and the message about it. Believers are His presbeuo, ambassadors, who speak with the King's authority. And the climax is dikaiosyne, righteousness: the sinless Christ made sin so that in Him we become the righteousness of God.
basic.typology: The new creation fulfils the prophetic promise of new heavens and a new earth and echoes the first creation, now begun in those united to Christ. The sinless one made sin fulfils the sin offering and the scapegoat of the Day of Atonement, on whom the guilt of the people was laid, and answers the pattern of substitution running through the whole sacrificial system.
basic.christ: Christ is everything here. His love compels; His death for all is the turning point of history; His resurrection gives believers a new life to live for Him. He is the one in whom there is new creation, the one through whom God reconciles the world, and above all the one who knew no sin yet was made sin for us. In Him, and only in Him, we become the righteousness of God. The great exchange is the summit of the passage.
basic.god: God is the reconciler, the offended party who Himself provides the way back, not counting our trespasses against us. He is the author of the whole work, from which all this comes, and the one who makes His appeal through His ambassadors. In verse 21 He is the one who made Christ to be sin and who credits His righteousness to us, both just and the justifier of those who are in Christ.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 5 says while we were enemies we were reconciled to God through the death of His Son. Isaiah 53 speaks of the servant on whom the LORD laid the iniquity of us all. Galatians 6 speaks of the new creation. Philippians 3 speaks of a righteousness from God that depends on faith.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible tells how a holy God reconciles a rebellious world to Himself. Here the mystery is unveiled: God in Christ was reconciling the world, laying our sin on the sinless one and giving us His righteousness. Those in Christ are the new creation, the first light of the new world God has promised, and the church is sent as His ambassadors, imploring a lost world to be reconciled to God.

context.literary: This section flows from the judgment seat of verse 10 and gathers up the themes of the whole letter into a gospel summary. The fear of the Lord and the love of Christ frame Paul's motive; the new creation and reconciliation state the gospel; and verses 20 to 21 turn doctrine into direct appeal. Verse 21 is a tightly balanced sentence, the sinless made sin, the guilty made righteous, and forms the climax.
context.historical: An ambassador in the Roman world carried the full authority of the one who sent him, and his words were received as the sovereign's own. Paul's rivals prized outward appearances and impressive credentials; Paul answers that his ministry is validated not by appearances but by the gospel he carries as Christ's envoy. The imagery of reconciliation drew on the ending of enmity between estranged parties or nations.
context.canonical: Verse 21 stands with Isaiah 53 and Romans 3 to 5 as a fountainhead of the doctrine of substitutionary atonement and justification. The new creation language links Genesis, Isaiah 65, and Revelation 21, while the ministry of reconciliation defines the mission of the church, echoed wherever the New Testament speaks of proclaiming the gospel to the world.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ground Paul's ministry and his appeal to the Corinthians in the gospel itself, showing that the love of Christ, His death for all, and the reconciling work of God compel both his labour and their response, and to state with unmatched clarity the great exchange by which sinners are made the righteousness of God in Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes what God has accomplished in Christ, which is settled truth, and on that basis issues a prescriptive appeal, be reconciled to God, and a call to live no longer for self but for Christ. The doctrine grounds the summons; the summons applies the doctrine.

debates.question: What does it mean that God made Him to be sin for us?

views.label: Christ bore our sin as our substitute, our guilt imputed to Him
views.case: The historic reading is that God reckoned our sin to the sinless Christ, who bore its penalty in our place, so that His righteousness is reckoned to us; the balanced sentence of verse 21 supports this double imputation.

views.label: Christ was made a sin offering
views.case: The same Greek word can mean sin offering, as the BSB footnote notes, so some render it that God made Christ a sin offering, drawing on the sacrificial system; this still affirms substitution, that the offering bears sin in the sinner's place.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm penal substitution, that the sinless Christ dealt with our sin in our place; the imputation reading foregrounds the exchange that gives us God's own righteousness.

debates.question: How wide is the reconciliation when Paul says God was reconciling the world?

views.label: The gospel is offered to the whole world and accomplished for all who are in Christ
views.case: The world names the scope of the gospel's offer and Christ's sufficient work, while the actual reconciliation is received by faith, be reconciled to God, so the appeal is universal and the enjoyment is by union with Christ.

views.label: World means people of every kind, Jew and Gentile alike
views.case: Others stress that world underlines that reconciliation is not limited to Israel but reaches all nations, so no group is excluded from the message of reconciliation.

debates.bearing: Either way the message goes to all without distinction and is received by being reconciled to God through faith in Christ, so the ambassadors' appeal is pressed on everyone.

study.originalLanguages: The passage turns on the language of reconciliation. God katallasso, reconciles, estranged sinners to Himself through Christ, taking the whole initiative, and He gives the katallage, the reconciliation, which is both the accomplished peace and the message that proclaims it. On that basis believers become presbeuo, ambassadors for Christ, envoys who carry the King's own word so that God makes His appeal through them. And the summit is dikaiosyne, righteousness: God made the sinless Christ to be sin on our behalf so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God, our sin reckoned to Him and His righteousness reckoned to us, the great exchange at the heart of the gospel.

typology.type: The sin offering and the scapegoat
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, made sin for us, on whom our guilt was laid as the sacrifices of the Day of Atonement foreshadowed.
Begun in those united to Christ and fulfilling the prophetic promise of new heavens and a new earth, consummated in Revelation 21.
The servant on whom the LORD laid the iniquity of us all is fulfilled in Christ made sin so that we might be counted righteous.

study.christ: Christ stands at the centre of every line. His love compels the whole ministry; His death for all is the hinge on which the passage turns; His resurrection gives His people a new life to live no longer for themselves but for Him. He is the one in whom there is new creation and through whom God reconciles the world. Supremely He is the sinless one whom God made to be sin for us, so that in Him, by a wondrous exchange, we become the righteousness of God. The passage exists to display Him.
study.god: God is the reconciler who takes the initiative, the offended party who Himself provides the way back and refuses to count our trespasses against us. Everything flows from Him: the new creation, the ministry, the message, the appeal He makes through His ambassadors. In verse 21 He is the God who laid our sin on the sinless Christ and credits Christ's righteousness to us, so that He is shown to be both perfectly just and the justifier of all who are in Christ. The whole work is His, and to Him belongs the glory.

crossReferences.note: While we were enemies we were reconciled to God through the death of His Son, the reconciliation Paul proclaims here.
The LORD laid on the servant the iniquity of us all, foreshadowing Christ made sin for us.
Neither circumcision nor uncircumcision counts for anything, but a new creation, echoing verse 17.
A righteousness from God that depends on faith, parallel to becoming the righteousness of God in Christ.

study.oneStory: The whole of Scripture tells how a holy God reconciles a rebel world to Himself. Here the mystery is laid bare: in Christ, God was reconciling the world, laying our sin on the sinless one and clothing us with His own righteousness. Those in Christ are the new creation, the dawn of the new world the prophets promised, and the church is sent as God's ambassadors, imploring a lost world to be reconciled to God. The account runs from the enmity of the fall to the peace secured at the cross and consummated in the new creation.

quiz.q: What compels Paul in his ministry, according to verse 14?

options.text: The love of Christ, who died for all so that all died.
options.note: Verse 14 names Christ's love as the compelling motive of his ministry.

options.text: His fear of failing the Corinthian church.
options.note: He does fear the Lord, but the compelling force he names is Christ's love.

options.text: His desire to prove himself against his rivals.
options.note: Paul refuses to boast in appearances; Christ's love drives him.

options.text: The reward he expects for his labours.
options.note: His motive is the love of Christ, not personal reward.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by a new creation in verse 17?

options.text: Anyone united to Christ is made new; the old has passed and the new has come.
options.note: Verse 17 declares a real new creation for everyone in Christ.

options.text: A person who has simply turned over a new leaf by effort.
options.note: It is God's creative work in Christ, not mere self improvement.

options.text: Only the apostles are made new in this way.
options.note: It is true of anyone who is in Christ, not apostles alone.

options.text: A promise only for the age to come, with no reality now.
options.note: The new has already come for those in Christ.

options.text: God reconciles us to Himself through Christ, not counting our trespasses against us.
options.note: Verses 18 to 19 make God the reconciler who acts through Christ.

options.text: We reconcile ourselves to God by making amends.
options.note: Reconciliation is God's initiative through Christ, not our amends.

options.text: God and sinners each meet halfway to settle the matter.
options.note: God provides the whole way back; it is not a meeting halfway.

options.text: Reconciliation happens automatically for everyone regardless.
options.note: The message must be received: be reconciled to God.

quiz.q: What is the great exchange described in verse 21?

options.text: The sinless Christ was made sin for us, so that in Him we become the righteousness of God.
options.note: Verse 21 states the double exchange at the heart of the gospel.

options.text: Christ set a perfect example we must now copy to be righteous.
options.note: It is imputation, not merely an example; His righteousness becomes ours.

options.text: God overlooked sin without any cost being paid.
options.note: The cost was borne by Christ, made sin in our place.

options.text: We earn righteousness by our own obedience over time.
options.note: The righteousness is God's, received in Christ, not earned.

questions: Paul says the love of Christ compels him. What would change if the love of Christ, rather than duty or fear, became the driving force of your life?
One died for all, so those who live should live no longer for themselves but for Him. Where are you still living for yourself rather than for Christ?
In Christ you are a new creation; the old has passed away. What old identity or pattern do you need to stop treating as still true of you?
God has made every believer an ambassador with the message of reconciliation. Who in your life needs to hear the appeal, be reconciled to God, from you?
God made the sinless Christ to be sin so that you might become the righteousness of God. How does resting in that exchange free you from trying to establish your own righteousness?

translationNotes.note: The phrase rendered new creation can also be rendered new creature; either way it declares a real, God wrought newness for everyone united to Christ.

God was reconciling the world to Himself in Christ describes God as the reconciler who acts through Christ and does not count trespasses; the world marks the universal reach of the message received by faith.

Made Him to be sin can also be rendered a sin offering, as the BSB footnote observes; both readings affirm penal substitution, and the balanced sentence supports the exchange in which our sin is reckoned to Christ and His righteousness, dikaiosyne, to us.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Corinthians 5:11-21. Footnote markers were omitted from the verse text. No em dashes were present in this passage; none introduced.
